data:
  prometheus.yml: |
    global:
      scrape_interval: 15s
    scrape_configs:
      - job_name: "prometheus"
        static_configs:
          - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
  rabbitmq_rules.yml: |
    groups:
      - name: rabbitmq
        rules:

            expr: up{job="rabbitmq"}

content.thinking:
I can see the issue now. The Prometheus config only has a scrape job for itself (`localhost:9090`), not for RabbitMQ. I need to:

1. Update the Prometheus ConfigMap to add a scrape job for RabbitMQ pods on port 15692
2. The RabbitMQ metrics are exposed on port 15692 with `/metrics`

The best approach for scraping all 3 RabbitMQ pods is to use Kubernetes service discovery. Since we can't use CRDs or operators, I'll use the native Prometheus Kubernetes SD configuration (`kubernetes_sd_configs`) with `role: pod` to discover all 3 RabbitMQ pods.
Alternatively, I could leverage the headless service to directly target each pod's DNS entry through static configuration. Let me check the Prometheus deployment to see what port it's listening on and what arguments it's using.

content.thinking:
Now I understand the setup. The Prometheus is listening on port 9090 (containerPort), but the Service exposes it on 8000. The Prometheus config is missing the RabbitMQ scrape job.

The RabbitMQ metrics are on port 15692 with `/metrics`. There's a headless service `rabbitmq-headless` which should give DNS entries for each pod like:
- `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:15692`
- `rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:15692`
- `rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:15692`

I need to update the `prometheus-config` ConfigMap to add a RabbitMQ scrape job that targets all three pods via the headless service DNS entries, with the job name set to "rabbitmq".

I could use static configs with the DNS names or leverage Kubernetes service discovery with pod role. Since Prometheus has `--web.enable-lifecycle` enabled, I can reload the config dynamically without restarting. I'll go with Kubernetes SD to automatically discover RabbitMQ pods based on their labels, so I should check what labels are on the RabbitMQ pods first.

content.thinking:
The existing ClusterRoleBinding for `prometheus` is bound to the `prometheus` service account in the `monitoring` namespace, NOT in our namespace. The Prometheus pod is using the `default` service account.

I have two options:
1. Use static_configs with the headless service DNS entries (simplest, doesn't need RBAC)
2. Set up proper RBAC for kubernetes_sd_configs (more complex but more robust)

Since the headless service `rabbitmq-headless` already exists and gives DNS entries like `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:15692`, I can use static_configs targeting all 3 pod DNS names. This is simpler and doesn't require RBAC.
I'll go with static_configs and manually specify the three RabbitMQ pod endpoints using their fully qualified DNS names from the headless service.

The job name needs to be "rabbitmq" to match what the recording rules expect. I also notice the prometheus.yml config doesn't currently reference the rabbitmq_rules.yml file, so I should add a rule_files section pointing to it at the mounted path.

Now I need to update the ConfigMap to add the scrape job for RabbitMQ with the three pod DNS names in static_configs. Since the Prometheus deployment has lifecycle reloading enabled, I can trigger a config reload via the reload endpoint instead of restarting the pod—I'll use kubectl exec to hit that from within the container.

Good. The service maps port 8000 → container port 9090. So Prometheus internally is at port 9090.

Now let me update the ConfigMap to:
1. Add `rule_files` section to reference `rabbitmq_rules.yml`
2. Add the RabbitMQ scrape job targeting all 3 pods via the headless service

Then I'll trigger a config reload via `kubectl exec` into the Prometheus pod and hit `localhost:9090/-/reload`.

content.text: The current Prometheus config is missing the RabbitMQ scrape job entirely. I'll update the ConfigMap to add a scrape job using the headless service DNS names for all 3 pods, then trigger a hot reload.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-03-2026-8301ac7b-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: prometheus-config
  namespace: karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-03-2026-8301ac7b-cluster-a
data:
  prometheus.yml: |
    global:
      scrape_interval: 15s
    rule_files:
      - /etc/prometheus/rabbitmq_rules.yml
    scrape_configs:
      - job_name: "prometheus"
        static_configs:
          - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
      - job_name: "rabbitmq"
        metrics_path: /metrics
        static_configs:
          - targets:
              - "rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-03-2026-8301ac7b-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
              - "rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-03-2026-8301ac7b-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
              - "rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-03-2026-8301ac7b-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
  rabbitmq_rules.yml: |
    groups:
      - name: rabbitmq
        rules:
          - record: rabbitmq_up
            expr: up{job="rabbitmq"}
EOF
input.description: Apply updated Prometheus ConfigMap with RabbitMQ scrape job

content.text: Now trigger a hot reload of Prometheus config (it has `--web.enable-lifecycle` enabled):

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-03-2026-8301ac7b-cluster-a"
PROM_POD=$(kubectl -n $NS get pod -l app=prometheus -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
echo "Prometheus pod: $PROM_POD"
kubectl -n $NS exec $PROM_POD -- wget -q -O- --post-data='' http://localhost:9090/-/reload
echo "reload exit: $?"

content.thinking: The reload was successful. Now let me wait a moment and then verify that Prometheus is scraping all 3 RabbitMQ targets.

content.text: Good, reload succeeded. Let me wait a few seconds for the first scrape and then verify the targets:

import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
targets = data.get('data', {}).get('activeTargets', [])
for t in targets:
    print(f\"job={t['labels'].get('job')} scrapeUrl={t['scrapeUrl']} health={t['health']}\")
"

content.thinking:
The Prometheus logs show that the config was reloaded (at 21:13:11), but I don't see any RabbitMQ targets in the target list. Let me check if the actual content of the ConfigMap was updated properly and if the Prometheus pod is reading the new config.

Wait, the reload happened but the target list only shows prometheus. Let me check the config that's actually loaded. Maybe the configmap was updated but the pod didn't pick up the new file because the configmap is volume-mounted and k8s takes time to sync the volume mount.
Looking at the reload timestamps more carefully, the initial load at 21:11:58 had the old config with no RabbitMQ targets, and the reload at 21:13:11 completed very quickly (730µs), which suggests the scrape manager accepted the new config but the volume mount inside the pod probably hasn't synced the updated ConfigMap data yet.

Kubernetes can take up to a minute to propagate ConfigMap changes to mounted volumes, so the reload likely happened before the actual file on disk was updated. I should check what the prometheus.yml file actually contains inside the pod right now to confirm whether it has the RabbitMQ targets or not.

content.text: The config was reloaded but the volume mount may not have updated yet. Let me check what Prometheus is actually reading:

content.content:
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
rule_files:
  - /etc/prometheus/rabbitmq_rules.yml
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: "prometheus"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
  - job_name: "rabbitmq"
    metrics_path: /metrics
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - "rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-03-2026-8301ac7b-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"

"
echo "---"
# Also confirm RabbitMQ pods still healthy
kubectl -n $NS get pods -l app=rabbitmq

content.thinking:
2 samples instead of 3. The recording rule fires based on the `up` metric, but the recording rule only fires once per evaluation interval (which is the same as the scrape_interval by default). Let me check why rabbitmq-0 isn't in the result.

Actually, the issue might be that the recording rule `rabbitmq_up = up{job="rabbitmq"}` is being evaluated and the rabbitmq-0 scrape data hasn't been evaluated into the recording rule yet. Or maybe there's a TSDB staleness window.

Wait, but the targets API shows all 3 are "up".
